did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a hybrid Deep Learning method based on the EfficientNet-B3 convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture to automate the classification of skin diseases. The model was trained on a dataset of 48,229 dermoscopic images across 15 classes of cancerous, precancerous, and non-cancerous skin lesions, achieving 94% classification accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing and proposing a hybrid Deep Learning method based on EfficientNet-B3 for multi-class skin disease classification. It details the methodology (preprocessing, data augmentation, model training on a dataset of 48,229 images), reports specific experimental results (94% accuracy, precision/recall/F1 scores), and includes a comparative analysis with existing models. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICECTE 2026), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: An Effective Hybrid EfficientNet-B3 Deep Learning Approach for Multi-Class Skin Disease Classification
journal: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Electrical, Computer & Telecommunication Engineering (ICECTE 2026)
brief_summary: This study proposes a hybrid deep learning approach based on the EfficientNet-B3 architecture to automate the multi-class classification of skin diseases. The model was trained and evaluated on a novel dataset comprising 48,229 dermoscopic images across 15 classes of cancerous, precancerous, and non-cancerous lesions. Preprocessing steps included hair removal via black-hat filtering, dataset balancing, oversampling, and data augmentation to enhance image quality and model generalization. The primary evaluation metric was classification accuracy, supported by precision, recall, and F1-scores. The proposed method achieved an impressive 94% overall accuracy, with macro and weighted average precision, recall, and F1-scores all averaging 0.94. Comparative analysis demonstrated that the EfficientNet-B3 model outperformed existing architectures such as MobileNetV2, VGG19, ResNet50, and DenseNet201.

all_task_types.target: Multi-class skin disease labels (15 classes including cancerous, precancerous, and non-cancerous lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper automates the classification of skin diseases using a hybrid EfficientNet-B3 model. The task is explicitly defined as 'multi-class skin disease classification' with 15 discrete diagnostic categories. Performance is evaluated using classification accuracy (94%), precision, recall, and F1-scores. The model predicts discrete class labels rather than continuous values.

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Skin Disease Detection Dataset
datasets_used.description: A novel dataset comprising 48,229 dermoscopic images of skin diseases, collected from Kaggle. It includes cancerous, precancerous, and non-cancerous lesions.

use.training: Used for training the EfficientNet-B3 model after preprocessing (hair removal, balancing, oversampling, and augmentation). The dataset was split into training (80%) and validation (20%) sets initially, with the training set further split into training and test sets in an 80:20 ratio.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process to monitor performance and prevent overfitting.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the model's performance (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) after the training set was split into training and test subsets.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using classification accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-scores, with comparative analysis based on these metrics without statistical significance testing.

Kaggle Skin Disease Detection Dataset (1.notes: The proposed EfficientNet-B3 model achieved 94% overall accuracy. Classes 3 (Chickenpox), 9 (Normal Skin), 11 (Ringworm), and 14 (Vascular Lesion) achieved perfect scores (1.00) in precision, recall, and F1-score. Classes 6 (Melanoma) and 10 (Pigmented Benign Keratosis) showed minor confusion due to visual similarities. The comparative analysis in Table III lists EfficientNetB3 at 93% accuracy, while the proposed hybrid approach achieved 94.00%.

architecture_details.scaling_method: Compound scaling (proportionally adjusts depth, width, and input resolution)
custom_layers: Five convolutional layers blocks with filter sizes 32, 64, 128, 256, 512
3x3 kernels with ReLU activation and same padding
Final output layer with 15 units and Softmax activation

callbacks: Model check pointing (retain best-performing model on validation set)

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layer followed by Softmax

preprocessing_techniques: Hair removal via black-hat morphological filtering (Closing operation followed by subtraction from original image) to eliminate noise from hair artifacts.
Dataset balancing using random oversampling technique applied to the training set to address class imbalance.
Real-time data augmentation including random zoom (zoom_range=2), random rotation (rotation_range=90), and other variations to enhance image quality and model generalization.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the training dataset to balance class distribution. Classes with fewer than 2546 images were oversampled to reach 2546 images (e.g., Acne from 2298 to 2546, Melanoma from 2045 to 2546). Nevus remained at 2546.
Randomly zooms into the image by up to a factor of 2 (zoom_range=2).
Randomly rotates images by up to 90 degrees (rotation_range=90).

conclusions: The proposed hybrid EfficientNet-B3 deep learning approach achieved an overall classification accuracy of 94% on a dataset of 48,229 dermoscopic images across 15 classes of cancerous, precancerous, and non-cancerous skin lesions.
The model demonstrated strong balance between sensitivity and specificity, with macro and weighted average precision, recall, and F1-scores all averaging 0.94.
The proposed EfficientNet-B3 model outperformed existing architectures including MobileNetV2 (91%), VGG19 (91%), ResNet50 (93%), and DenseNet201 (93%).

Specific classes such as Chickenpox (class 3), Normal Skin (class 9), Ringworm (class 11), and Vascular Lesion (class 14) achieved perfect classification scores (1.00) in precision, recall, and F1-score.

Classes with lower performance, including Dyshidrotic Eczema (class 5), Acne (class 0), and Nail Fungus (class 7), had F1-scores ranging between 0.80 and 0.90, attributed to high visual similarity among lesions and class imbalance.

Preprocessing techniques, specifically hair removal via black-hat filtering, dataset balancing, oversampling, and real-time data augmentation, were critical for enhancing image quality, model generalization, and preventing overfitting.
The model successfully avoided overfitting, as evidenced by smooth convergence of training and validation loss curves without significant divergence.
The study recommends the use of this automated detection method for the early detection of skin cancer to improve diagnostic accessibility and save lives.
important_remarks: The dataset is described as 'novel' and 'realistic' but sourced from Kaggle; specific provenance, collection protocols, and demographic diversity of the 48,229 images are not detailed, raising concerns about potential selection bias.
Three of the 15 classes (classes 8, 12, and 13) are labeled as 'Unknown' in the results, indicating incomplete class annotation or reporting in the study.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) for the performance differences between the proposed model and baselines.

Performance on clinically critical classes like Melanoma (class 6) and Pigmented Benign Keratosis (class 10) was lower (F1=0.80 and 0.82 respectively) due to visual similarity, which is a significant caveat for diagnostic reliability.
The study lacks external validation on independent, multi-center clinical datasets, limiting the generalizability of the 94% accuracy claim to real-world clinical settings.

Data leakage risk exists as the dataset was split into training/validation (80:20) and then the training set was further split into training/test (80:20), rather than using a held-out external test set.
The model was trained on a limited input resolution of 64x64 pixels, which may discard fine-grained dermatological features crucial for accurate diagnosis of complex lesions.
The study is published in a conference proceeding (ICECTE 2026) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may imply less rigorous scrutiny of the methodology and results.
